gold_final_answer: a_n = n + C for some nonnegative integer C
topics_flat: Algebra > Algebraic Expressions > Sequences and Series > Recurrence relations
Algebra > Equations and Inequalities > Linear and quadratic inequalities
Number Theory > Divisibility / Factorization > Greatest common divisors (gcd)
prompt:
Let $p \neq q$ be coprime positive integers. Determine all infinite sequences $a_{1}, a_{2}, \ldots$ of positive integers such that the following conditions hold for all $n \geqslant 1$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \max \left(a_{n}, a_{n+1}, \ldots, a_{n+p}\right)-\min \left(a_{n}, a_{n+1}, \ldots, a_{n+p}\right)=p \quad \text{ and } \\
& \max \left(a_{n}, a_{n+1}, \ldots, a_{n+q}\right)-\min \left(a_{n}, a_{n+1}, \ldots, a_{n+q}\right)=q .
\end{aligned}
$$

Then q≥2 coprime to 1, so q condition forces monotonicity over q+1 windows, hence entire sequence monotonic.

**Solution sketch:**

From the first condition, over any window of $p+1$ terms the range is exactly $p$. Since consecutive differences must be at least $\pm 1$-ish, and more precisely the $p$ steps in such a window have $|a_{i+1}-a_i|\ge 1$... The key claim is that the sequence is strictly monotonic with step $\pm 1$.

Assume WLOG $q > p$, so $q \ge 2$. Consider a window of $q+1$ terms with range $q$. The $q$ consecutive differences are integers summing (in absolute value with signs) to put max and min exactly $q$ apart, and this forces each $|a_{i+1}-a_i|=1$ with **all differences of the same sign** throughout the window. Overlapping windows then force the entire sequence to be monotonic with step $\pm 1$.

A strictly decreasing sequence of positive integers can't be infinite, so the sequence is strictly increasing by 1:
$$a_n = a_1 + (n-1).$$

One checks this satisfies both conditions for any $a_1 \in \mathbb{Z}^+$.

Final answer: $a_n = n + c$ for some constant integer $c \ge 0$ (equivalently, $a_n = a_1 + n - 1$ with $a_1 \ge 1$).
